author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
A Treatise on the Gift of Perseverance.
Three Leading Points of the Pelagian Doctrine.
originalBook: A Treatise on the Gift of Perseverance.
title: Three Leading Points of the Pelagian Doctrine.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Read with a little more attention its exposition in the treatise of the blessed martyr Cyprian, which he wrote concerning this matter, the title of which is, On the Lord’s Prayer; and see how many years ago, and what sort of an antidote was prepared against those poisons which the Pelagians were one day to use.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: For there are three points, as you know, which the catholic Church chiefly maintains against them.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: One of these is, that the grace of God is not given according to our merits; because even every one of the merits of the righteous is God’s gift, and is conferred by God’s grace.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: The second is, that no one lives in this corruptible body, however righteous he may be, without sins of some kind.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: The third is, that man is born obnoxious to the first man’s sin, and bound by the chain of condemnation, unless the guilt which is contracted by generation be loosed by regeneration.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Of these three points, that which I have placed last is the only one that is not treated of in the above-named book of the glorious martyr; but of the two others the discourse there is of such perspicuity, that the above-named heretics, modern enemies of the grace of Christ, are found to have been convicted long before they were born.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Among these merits of the saints, then, which are no merits unless they are the gifts of God, he says that perseverance also is God’s gift, in these words: “We say, ‘Hallowed be Thy name;’ not that we ask for God that He may be hallowed by our prayers, but that we beseech of Him that His name may be hallowed in us.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: But by whom is God sanctified, since He Himself sanctifies?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: Well, because He says, Be ye holy because I also am holy, we ask and entreat that we, who were sanctified in baptism, may persevere in that which we have begun to be.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: And a little after, still arguing about that self-same matter, and teaching that we entreat perseverance from the Lord, which we could in no wise rightly and truly do unless it were His gift, he says: “We pray that this sanctification may abide in us; and because our Lord and Judge warns the man that was healed and quickened by Him to sin no more, lest a worse thing happen unto him, we make this supplication in our constant prayers; we ask this, day and night, that the sanctification and quickening which is received from the grace of God may be preserved by His protection.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: That teacher, therefore, understands that we are asking from Him for perseverance in sanctification, that is, that we should persevere in sanctification, when we who are sanctified say, “Hallowed be Thy name.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: For what else is it to ask for what we have already received, than that it be given to us also not to cease from its possession?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: As, therefore, the saint, when he asks God that he may be holy, is certainly asking that he may continue to be holy, so certainly the chaste person also, when he asks that he may be chaste, the continent that he may be continent, the righteous that he may be righteous, the pious that he may be pious, and the like,—which things, against the Pelagians, we maintain to be God’s gifts,—are asking, without doubt, that they may persevere in those good things which they have acknowledged that they have received.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Predestination of the Saints.
verses.text: And if they receive this, assuredly they also receive perseverance itself, the great gift of God, whereby His other gifts are preserved.
